text: The surgical team performs a left transperitoneal laparoscopic nephrectomy, inserting a 10mm port at the left iliac fossa, a 5mm camera port at the transumbilical point, and additional 5mm ports under the left costal margin and laterally for assistance. The surgeon carefully mobilizes the spleen, retracting it beyond its position to avoid bleeding, and cuts the peritoneal attachments on the lateral side to allow the spleen to fall away. The mesenteric fat appears egg yellow, contrasting with the salmon pink fat of the Gerota's fascia. The goal is to expose the psoas muscle at the upper pole, indicating sufficient spleen mobilization.

The surgeon continues mobilizing the bowel caudally using a grasper, ensuring traction is applied in the avascular plane. The pancreas is visible on the left side of the screen, and care is taken to avoid injury to this structure.

The surgeon continues the dissection during a left nephrectomy, focusing on freeing the upper pole of the kidney and mobilizing the bowel medially. The gyrotous fascia is opened to expose the psoas major muscle, confirming the correct anatomical location. The gonadal vein is visible, aiding in retraction without needing to be lifted.

The surgical view shows the kidney with the hilum surrounded by fat, while the posterior part is freed. A fixed retractor is inserted to lift the kidney, allowing the surgeon to use sharp dissection to isolate the hilum. The gyrotus fascia is opened anterior to the renal vein, revealing the adrenal vein. Lumbar veins are visible, with one clipped, and the gonadal vein is seen below. There is slight oozing in the surgical field.

The surgeon applies green Hem-o-lok clips to a lumbar vein, ensuring careful placement to avoid interference with future stapling. The gonadal vein is divided, and gentle suction reveals a tributary of the renal vein, likely another small lumbar vein, which is dissected and controlled using additional Hem-o-lok clips.

The surgeon cleans the vein inferiorly and gently dissects the renal artery using scissors, removing fascia to expose the artery. A purple hemlock clip is placed on the renal artery, with two clips on the stay side and one at the top. The surgeon carefully avoids the adrenal vein while mobilizing the upper corner, opting to preserve the adrenal vein in this case. Gold hemlocks are used to secure the artery, ensuring they do not slip off.

The surgeon exposes the renal artery and clips it with purple Hemlocks, avoiding metal clips due to concerns about slippage. The remaining band of tissue around the hilum is clipped, and the kidney is separated from the adrenal gland. The Gerota's fascia is opened at the upper pole, revealing ample space as the spleen is retracted. The surgeon uses a vascular stapler and Hemlocks to control blood vessels, increasing diathermy settings to reduce bleeding. The gonadal vein is visible, and the ureter is clipped, completing the dissection.

The surgeon continues the nephrectomy by dissecting around the kidney, focusing on maintaining the correct tissue plane to avoid entering the abdominal wall. High diathermy settings are used to efficiently cut through the peritoneum while ensuring the kidney is completely free before attempting to bag it. The kidney is positioned to slide into the retrieval bag smoothly, with careful traction applied to avoid damaging any small arteries.
